coredata.dc:title: Naked eye colorimetric detection of fluoride through TiO2 NPs/CQDs based detector
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Singh, Neha
Kumari, Sonu
Khan, Suphiya
coredata.dc:description: Fluoride (F−) occurs naturally in a soil, air and water as commonly found element in earth crusts. Almost 200 million populations worldwide and approximately 60 million in India are facing F− contamination problem. Therefore, development of low-cost and highly sensitive colorimetric detector for F− ions using carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and metal salts is required. For this, CQDs were synthesized using flower waste from temples by green method and further characterized. CQDs-TiO2 complex solution color changes from green to black on addition of F− ions due to exchange of F− ions with hydroxyl ions of the solution. Absorption spectrum of CQDs-TiO2 complex solution depicts change in spectral graph on adding F− ions with the appearance of new band at approx. 550 nm. The color spectral as a function of F− ions concentration gave a linear response in the range of 2–8 ppm (R2 = 0.9808). The color change can be observed at 2 ppm after incubation of 5 min by naked eye. Developed colorimetric detector shows high selectivity with other ions (Cl−, Br−, I− and H2PO4 −). The F− ion detection was done in different water samples collected from districts of Haryana using developed detector. The developed colorimetric detector allows wide range of F− sensing for water sample even at every low concentration i.e 2 ppm.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Adsorption
Carbon-quantum dots (CQDs)
Colorimetric
Detector
Fluoride

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of TiO2 NPs and CQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FESEM of TiO2 NPs, (b) EDX spectrum of TiO2 NPs, (c) Photograph of as-prepared CQDs solution upon irradiation with 365 nm UV light, and (d) EDX spectrum of CQDs.
(a) XRD patterns of TiO2 NPs, and (b) XRD patterns of CQDs.
(a) Emission spectra at progressively increasing excitation wavelengths from 325 nm to 450 nm and excitation spectrum of the CQDs solution, and (b) Zeta Potential of TiO2 NPs.

(i) Color change in CQDs-TiO2 complex solution after addition of F− ions (a) CQDs-TiO2 complex solution, (b) 2–4 ppm, (c) 4–7 ppm, (d) 7 ppm and above, and (ii) Change in color of CQDs-TiO2 complex solution after addition of anion solutions (a) F− ions, (b) Cl− ions, (c) Br− ions, (d) I− ions and (e) H2PO4 − ions.

(a) Change in absorbance of CQDs-TiO2 complex solution upon addition of F− and other anionic solutions, and (b) Values of (A550/300) of CQDs-TiO2 complex solution upon addition of 100 ppm F− and other 100 anions. Error bars represent standard deviations based on three independent measurements.
(a) UV–vis spectra of CQDs-TiO2 complex solution in presence of increasing F− ions concentration, and (b) absorbance dependence of F− ions.
(a) Color change of CQDs-TiO2 complex solution with addition of water samples collected from different districts of Haryana, and (b) absorbance spectra of collected water samples.
Chemical reaction to oxidise the hydroxyl group into carboxyl group.
Nucleophilic substitution reaction to form an ester.
Schematic representation of possible mechanism for F− detection through developed colorimetric detector.
Comparison of F-detection capacity of TiO2 NPs/ CQDs based detector with other detectors.

head.ce:title: Naked eye colorimetric detection of fluoride through TiO2 NPs/CQDs based detector

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Bioscience and Biotechnology, Banasthali Vidyapith, Banasthali Tonk, Rajasthan 304022, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Bioscience and Biotechnology, Banasthali Vidyapith, Banasthali Tonk, Rajasthan 304022, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Bioscience and Biotechnology Banasthali University, P.O. Banasthali Vidyapith, Rajasthan 304022, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluoride (F−) occurs naturally in a soil, air and water as commonly found element in earth crusts. Almost 200 million populations worldwide and approximately 60 million in India are facing F− contamination problem. Therefore, development of low-cost and highly sensitive colorimetric detector for F− ions using carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and metal salts is required. For this, CQDs were synthesized using flower waste from temples by green method and further characterized. CQDs-TiO2 complex solution color changes from green to black on addition of F− ions due to exchange of F− ions with hydroxyl ions of the solution. Absorption spectrum of CQDs-TiO2 complex solution depicts change in spectral graph on adding F− ions with the appearance of new band at approx. 550 nm. The color spectral as a function of F− ions concentration gave a linear response in the range of 2–8 ppm (R2 = 0.9808). The color change can be observed at 2 ppm after incubation of 5 min by naked eye. Developed colorimetric detector shows high selectivity with other ions (Cl−, Br−, I− and H2PO4 −). The F− ion detection was done in different water samples collected from districts of Haryana using developed detector. The developed colorimetric detector allows wide range of F− sensing for water sample even at every low concentration i.e 2 ppm.

ce:section.ce:para: To date, nanomaterials have gained much attention with possible application in fluorescence and colorimetric sensors. Among them, colorimetric sensors have attracted tremendous attention as are highly sensitive, cost-effective and naked eye sensors [6]. The visible colorimetric sensor mechanism is based on metal–ligand coordination where it behaves as electronic acceptor and donor [24]. Several metal nanoparticles (NPs) are being used for detecting ions with colorimetry from aqueous solution. Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are utilized for sensors development because of their distinctive optical properties as higher stability, less toxicity, photoluminescence depending on the size, photobleaching resistance and easy bioconjugation [26,11]. Typically, CQDs have been synthesized through ultrasonic treatment, electrochemical oxidation, vapor deposition and green synthesis [13,10,21]. Among all, green chemistry showed advantageous properties as no toxic chemicals required, minimum waste produced, less time consuming, no high temperature required, cost-effective and eco-friendly [5,17].

Among different ionic species, F− is the major contributor of water pollution; globally affecting 200 million people [9]. It has been reported that F− causes dental fluorosis, osteoporosis, thyroid problem, growth suppression and sometimes mortality [20]. Drinking water systems, agricultural soils have a great involvement of F−. Almost 60 million people in and 15 provinces in India are highly affected by different kinds of fluorosis problems [7]. However, detectors available are generally developed through hazardous chemical protocols. Therefore, considering the role of F− in human life, it is essential to develop simple, selective, cost-effective, eco-friendly and efficient colorimetric detector for F− analysis.

Currently, flower waste is the major problem India is facing as festivals, marriages and many other occasions are celebrated round of the year. Various sources are marriages, temple, dargah, churches, gardens and other culture and religious ceremonies. Annually, approximately 80, 00,000 tons of flowers are dumped in Indian rivers [15]. Other than this, roughly 100 million roses on Valentine's Day were put on the market by Flowerpetal.com in U.S. [15]. This showed the high amount of flower waste produced at temples and other places and occasions.

In the present paper, we have designed a CQDs-TiO2 NPs based colorimetric detector that can rapidly detect F− ions in the water samples. Both CQDs and TiO2 NPs were synthesized through green method using waste flowers collected from temples as it proved to be cost-effective, rapid and environmental friendly method. To confirm the efficiency, we have done the F− sensitivity through color detection using different ions. To the best of our knowledge, the developed sensitive and affordable colorimetric would be the first approach for F− sensing using CQDs-TiO2 NPs based detector.

Analytical grade reagents were used without any further purification. Flower waste was collected from temples of Jaipur, Rajasthan (India). Dihydrogen phosphate (H2PO4), cupric sulfate (CuSO4), L-ascorbic acid (C6H8O6), titanium (IV) chloride were used as obtained from Sigma- Aldrich, India. Millipore milli-Q system was used for deionized water.

Waste flowers were chosen for the synthesis of TiO2 NPs because they are abundant and easily available as they are disposed after decoration and worship at temples. NPs were synthesised by green method as reported previously with slight modifications [22]. Firstly, waste flowers were washed with deionized water and chopped into fine pieces. 20 g flower waste was taken into an Erlenmeyer flask containing 60 ml of Milli-Q water. Next, solution was heated to 80 °C for 30 min and then supernatant was filtered with Whatmann No. 1 paper to get flower extract and kept at 4 °C for further use. The filtrate which was obtained was utilized as a reducing and stabilizer agent for NPs synthesis.

For TiO2 NPs synthesis, titanium (IV) chloride (TiCl4) solution of 0.01 M was freshly prepared for reduction process. Then, 10 ml extracted flower waste was added with 20 ml of 0.01 M TiCl4 solution and stirred continuously at 60 °C. TiCl4 to Ti+ ions complete reduction was confirmed when the color changed to brown. Further, Centrifugation was performed for the suspension for 20 min at 10,000 rpm and the pellet which was obtained, was washed with millipore water repeatedly and then dried in oven at 100 °C.

CQDs were also synthesised via green route as reported below with fewer alterations [4]. Briefly, 10 g of flower waste was taken and cleaned thoroughly using distilled water. Then, flower waste properly crushed in mortar pestle to get fine paste and the volume was make upto 50 ml with distilled water. Then, the mixture was heated at 80 °C for 2 hrs and filtered with Whatmann No. 1 paper to get brown colored solution. Further, centrifugation was done at 4500 rpm for 10 min and was taken by decantation. The obtained solution was then refluxed at 80 °C for 2 hrs and allowed to cool. Then, centrifuge for 10 min at 4500 rpm and obtained solution was suspended with 1 N 5 ml NaOH [4]. The obtained CQDs solution was then observed under UV light for fluorescence and stored at 4 °C for further use. Finally, CQDs were characterized through UV–visible spectrophotometer, Energy dispersive X-ray analysis (EDX), X-ray diffraction (XRD) and fluorescence spectrophotometer.

The surface morphology of TiO2 NPs was acquired by FESEM (MIRA 3 TESCAN). Elemental composition of TiO2 NPs and CQDs was identified by EDX attached with the FESEM. Crystalline structure and purity of both were distinguished using an XRD (Bruker D8 Discover X-Ray diffractometer). The surface charge of TiO2 NPs was obtained by the zeta (ζ) potential. By titrating the ζ-potential over the pH range of 2–9, the isoelectric point of NPs was achieved. A fluorescence spectrum of CQDs was measured on PerkinElmer LS 45 spectrophotometer.

For the F− ion sensing, TiO2 NPs based colorimetric sensor was developed. Briefly, F− stock solution (100 mg L−1) was prepared by addition of 0.0221 g NaF to 100 ml double distilled water. Colorimetric detection of F− ions was done to identify the selectivity and low concentration limit of detector. Firstly, CQDs-TiO2 complex solution was prepared with composition of 30 μL CQDs (1 mg ml−1), 500 μL TiO2NPs, 500 μL H2PO4, 500 μL CuSO4 and 1.5 ml C6H8O6 (1.5 g in 50 ml distilled water for all) till solution color become green. Then, 500 μL of CQDs-TiO2 complex solution was mixed with 300 μL of different concentrations of F− ions. For the detection, 300 μL of different concentrations of F− ion (2 ppm, 4 ppm, and 6 ppm and above) was added in 500 μL CQDs-TiO2 complex solution (CQDs-TiO2 NPs-H2PO4-CuSO4-C6H8O6) to identify the lowest naked eye detection limit. After 5 min incubation, color of solution changes from green to black and observed by naked eye. Selectivity tests for F− ions were carried with some other anions. All selectivity tests were carried out with some other anions such as chloride (Cl−), phosphate (P−), bromide (Br−) and iodide (I−) (100 ppm). UV–vis spectrum was also recorded for different F− ion concentrations.

The selectivity of the F− ion detector was investigated by checking its absorption maxima on interaction with other anions such Cl−, H2PO4 −, Br− and I−. To confirm the precision and recovery of the detector, each set of experiments was carried out in triplicate. Similar results were obtained within the maximum error of 2–3%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of TiO2 NPs and CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The surface morphology of synthesized TiO2 NPs was analyzed through FESEM (Fig. 1 a). TiO2 NPs showed dense agglomeration of particles and are irregular in shape [1]. TiO2 NPs elemental composition was obtained through EDX elemental mapping and showed the presence of Ti (17.1%) and O (18.1%) (Fig. 1b). EDX elemental spectrum confirmed the presence of Ti element in the sample. CQDs solution shows light–brown color under the normal light and emitted blue luminescence on exposure with 365 nm UV irradiation as shown in Fig. 1c. CQDs sample EDX analysis showed the presence of C (94.7%), O (3.5%), Na (0.2%) and Cl (0.3%) (Fig. 1d).

Fig. 2 illustrates the XRD pattern of TiO2 NPs and CQDs; intense diffraction peaks of TiO2 NPs at 25.33°, 27.50°, 37.88°, 48.13° and of CQDs at 30.10° were observed [2]. The XRD patterns indicate the crystalline nature of both and their average crystalline size can be determined using through Debye-Scherrer equation. Debye-Scherrer equation is shown as:
(1) D = kλ/ βhkl cosθhkl
where D is the crystallite size, λ is the X-ray wavelength of radiation for Cu Kα (0.154 nm), βhkl is the full-width at half maximum (FWHM), k is Scherrer constant (0.9) and θhkl is the diffraction angle. The average crystallite size of TiO2 NPs was 32.72 nm and CQDs was 4.94 nm. The XRD peaks of TiO2 NPs were observed as similar to JCPDS No.84-1285. CQDs synthesized through waste material exhibits photoluminescence properties and its strong photoluminescence and optical properties were confirmed (Fig. 3 a). At 457 nm, maximum emission was observed when excited at 350 nm [2].

TiO2 NPs surface charge plays a prominent role in their moving ability and stability of suspension in drinking water. The level of electrostatic repulsion between particles is decided by the extent of surface potential. The ζ-potential is positive for TiO2 NPs, when the isoelectric point (IEP) of 6.4 is higher than the pH and positive surface charges appears over a broad range of pH (2 to 6) [27].

For practical application visual inspection of colorimetric detector is essential as it should be capable to detect and provide immediate color change response to F− ions. CQDs have been attracting attention in sensing of F− ion and other heavy metals as emission of CQDs are controlled by surface states and different emission centres on the surface result in excitation dependent emission [8]. The potential application of CQDs-TiO2 complex solution based colorimetric detector can be validated by selectivity mechanism of the detector. This can be accomplish by examining the color response of CQDs-TiO2 complex solution upon addition of F− and other anions such as Cl−, Br−, I− and H2PO4 −. As shown in Fig. 4 i, distinct color change was observed from green to black upon addition of different F− concentrations (2 ppm-8 ppm).

Notable color change is observed on addition of 2 ppm F− solution in CQDs-TiO2 complex solution from green to grey. Then, color become more distinct as shown in result by addition of 3 ppm, 4 ppm, 5 ppm and above F− solution to mixture from grey to black, respectively. Hence, CQDs-TiO2 complex solution become notable sensitive towards F− ions at concentration as low as 2 ppm, as it not offer any color change with other anions such as Cl−, Br−, I− and H2PO4 − (Fig. 4ii).

For further evaluation of selectivity of CQDs-TiO2 complex solution for other ions, the adsorption titration of CQDs-TiO2 complex solution was done (Fig. 5 a). Maximum wavelength of CQDs-TiO2 complex solution was observed at about 300 nm that can be assigned to n-π* transition of the O-H groups present on the surface of the CQDs [2]. But with the addition of F− solution, new peak was observed at 550 nm. On titration with F− ions solution of high ppm, the absorbance intensity increases. Titration showed the variation in values of adsorption ratio (A550/300) for CQDs-TiO2 complex solution on addition of F− ions solution and other anions. Absorption ratio A550/300 variation of CQDs-TiO2 complex solution on addition of F− and other anions is shown in Fig. 5b. As observed in the Fig. 5b, only F− ions were reported with significant increase in A550/300 value. This suggests that the developed detector is highly selective for the F− ions in comparison to other ions.

Fig. 6 a shows the UV titration of CQDs-TiO2 complex solution upon addition of F− ions with different concentrations. Firstly, CQDs-TiO2 complex solution showed absorption band at around 300 nm in the adsorption spectrum as the carbon dots was attributed to n-π* transition [25]. But upon addition of different concentration of F− ions, a new band at 550 nm was observed with bathochromic shift of 183 nm attributed to the intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) transition between the CQDs-TiO2 complex solution and F− ion complex. The transition metal Cu accepts a lone pair in the vacant d-orbital or orbital of valence shell. The transition metals have very strong tendency to form complexes. Initially in the formed CQDs complex, the oxygen atom having lone pair act as ligand by the donation of lone pair. Then, we test the impure water sample having F− ions, here F− ion act as lone donor atom and further form complex with copper. A linear relationship between the R2-value and the concentrations of F− ions could be observed in the range of 2 ppm to 8 ppm (R2 = 0.980). Thus, F− ions concentration in different water could be identified through developed colorimetric detector.

TiO2NPs and CQDs gave excellent results in sensing F−. The comparison of performance of different colorimetric nanosensors for F− is in Table 1 . In view of the simplicity TiO2NPs/CQDs colorimetric nanosensors with LOD 0.057 µM, more desirable for F− detection with high sensitivity and selectivity.

Color change was observed in water samples collected from different districts of Haryana. Intense color was observed in water samples taken from Faridabad and Sonepat which showed the high F− ions concentration between 6 ppm and above. However, dark grey was observed in samples collected from Bhiwani and Rewari which concluded the F− concentration as 3-4 ppm and light grey color in water sample collected from Panipat (Fig. 7 ).

The potential mechanism of naked eye colorimetric detector for F− is described in Figs. 8a and 8b
. In the reactions, first the Titanium (IV) oxide acts as an oxidising agent because sequential onset of each reduced species indicate that Ti2+ is not stable in proximity to Ti4+ at room temperature, but rather exhibits a tendency to form a gradient for the Titanium [16]. So it oxidised the hydroxyl group (OH) into carboxyl group CO.
In the second step the carboxylic group undergoes nucleophilic substitution reaction with dihydrogen phosphate ion and form an inorganic ester.
The inorganic ester having charge on oxygen atom acts as a ligand and form a complex with Cu2+ ion and ascorbic acid.

This complex of Cu2+ ion having d9 electronic configuration show color in its solid state as well as in aqueous state, this is because of absorption of light radiations in visible region which cause d-d transition of electron from t2g orbital to eg orbital and show complementary color.

Further there is nucleophilic substitution reaction by the nucleophilic F− ion. In brief, nucleophilic substitution takes place when we add a sample of water having F− ion. The potential mechanism is shown by schematic representation in Fig. 9 .

In the current study, we have described an easy, green and simple method to synthesize colorimetric detector which detects F− ions over other anions in drinking water. Detection limit was found be very low as 2 ppm. The developed detector can be utilized for the easy detection of F− ions as it gives distinct color change above 2–8 ppm from green to black. Selectivity of developed detector with other anions such as Cl−, Br−, I− and H2PO4 − has done and no spectral response was observed. This detector is particularly helpful for places in India, where there F− is more than 2 ppm in groundwater. The developed portable, selective and sensitive CQDs-TiO2 based colorimetric detector is for F− ion detection. Further work is underway for developing test beads and strips. In near future we will develop more portable and sensitive detector for F− detection.

Neha Singh carried out the methodology and write the original draft of the manuscript. Sonu Kumari review and edit the manuscript. Suphiya Khan has provided her supervision and administrate the project.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was funded by DBT BIG-BIRAC Ref No. BIRAC/CCAMP0723/BIG-13/18 and DST major project entitled “Low Cost-Renewable Energy Driven (LC-RED) Water Treatment Solution Centre” Ref No. DST/TM/WTI/WIC/2K17/124. We acknowledged Prof. Aditya Shastri for providing research amenities and bioinformatics centre for providing computational facilities.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Langmuir
